---
title: "Working with time"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{working_with_time}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>"
)
```

```{r setup}
library(astrocoords)
```

This vignette shows a few simple ways to work with Julian Date (JD) and Modified Julian Date (MJD).

Astronomers often use JD for exact time stamps.

```{r}
now_utc <- as.POSIXct("2026-03-08 12:34:56", tz = "UTC")
datetime_to_jd(now_utc)
```

If you start from a calendar date without a clock time, the function uses midnight UTC.

```{r}
datetime_to_jd(as.Date("2026-03-08"))
```

You can also go back from JD to a normal R date-time.

```{r}
jd <- datetime_to_jd(now_utc)
jd_to_datetime(jd, tz = "UTC")
```

MJD is just a shifted version of JD. The package can convert it back to a normal date-time too.

```{r}
mjd_to_datetime(51544, tz = "UTC")
```
